speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 6, January 30, 1995. Survivor, Dr. Esther Fox, interviewer, Miriam Radner. Yes, this is tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: --not so stupid.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So this Mr. Fox wrote you a letter in 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He wrote all the time from '45. But in '47, all of the sudden, I get a letter-- not all of a sudden, to me it was an extra-- and he writes, I never hear from my family. It's already two years passed by. If you are not-- I remember exactly the wording-- if you are not emotionally involved with anybody, how about caring this broken ship together? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I read that, I was completely out. I didn't know what it means. I didn't know him. I knew because mentioned name, they said that he was a devoted father and a good husband. And they both were the same principals. They were two parallel schools because it was the same profession but there was so much attending students that they divided. But the curriculum and everything and the policy of the schools were the same and the two were best friends. So I knew from what people told me that he was a nice man and not an adventurer. Because I never know. So you have to come to the [INAUDIBLE] in America, rich woman, me. So I showed my roommate. We read everything together. And I don't know, what's that? I was not prepared for America, I wasn't prepared for anything. So but after three or four days when it digested, the politeness asked to answer, so I wrote to him that I received the letter, which made me-- whatever, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I am not involved emotionally and if you want to come, I wouldn't stop you. You might come. But I remember exactly I wrote that I write one special condition because you say A and you come here, you don't have to say B. This I wrote because I didn't say anything so I didn't say-- I didn't say-- I don't promise anything, but because I didn't-- it's not my initiative. But I wrote, if you said A, when you are here, you don't have to say B. So it didn't take long because the-- the 2nd of-- --February I have to look here on the ring, we got married. So he came. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because how could I come? I wasn't an American citizen. So he came-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and so my professor Wolff knew everything. I shared because they took care of me, you know. And so he came and I went to meet him at the train. So I didn't know him and he didn't know me, but all Swedes are tall, all are blonde. So everybody left and I stay and he stay. It must be, I and him. So I didn't have a rose. So [INAUDIBLE]. And my friend, one went to watch from the gallery. She wanted to see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember the first words he said to-- my husband to me, just for me, not too big, not too small. And I took-- you know, I rented a room in a hotel for him and so and so. And that-- and Professor Wolff invited him, they made a dinner over there, a big fuss. And he was-- he could talk English and the others could talk English, too. And we-- we talked and he was very warm and we were very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after-- in Sweden has to be, how do you call it, you have the announcement to get married. So after three or four weeks, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: By a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. Because only in the city hall. Professor Wolff was a little surprised. And I-- my husband was a traditional Jew more, but he was controversial. He said the rabbi is younger, he could be my son and he is married to a non-Jewish woman so he is making my marriage kosher? I make it kosher myself. And I didn't care because to me, it was a matter of trust. If I don't trust this with the marriage-- with the paper wouldn't help. And if you don't trust-- I trusted it. So we got married and he went right away back to-- not right away, but a very short while. Yeah. He went first to visit all the camps because he had messages to visit here, to visit there. And I was glad that he wants to go. He went and I worked still in the laboratory of Professor Wolff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he went to America, he right away started--

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh, you go. They bring my lunch. So-- I forgot. And-- take it from him. Tell that I cannot get up. But open the door. Wait a second. Hello. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he stopped it? No? Oh, so I cannot talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So tell me-- so he went to visit all the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he find--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Some people gave regards and they-- they had money for some people if he finds them. All right, this we did. So he went to America, I'm sure at the time, and he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he find his children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No children, no wife, nobody. And he was very emotional about all this and he cried all the time about his children. And he started to get the papers for me, an affidavit, and then I came to America the 17th of May, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came into New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he met you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And his family met me. And I couldn't talk because I didn't know English. But all right, so a little Yiddish with his mother. He had here his mother. He went to visit his mother for vacation and got stuck here because it was summer. So right after this, he introduced me to some Polish doctors who went through all the things which are supposed to be done here and I found out that there is a course that first you have to press examination of English language. So I inscribed for that and over there was a big crime because it was from all the universities-- not from Paris and not from Sweden, from Italy, from Vienna. And I took this course in English and there was a written examination and I passed. So before-- I passed. Later, I arrived and they started-- there was a course for passing the State Board, the medical examinations. At that time I don't know whether it existed because now it is, but anyhow, Paris is considered grade A so I didn't have to take any courses, only the examination. So I took the course, whatever it took, and I went to the examination, which is only written, no oral, and I passed at once, which is also a little unusual because it doesn't mean only that you know so much the medicine so well. Some people cannot write so fast because it's a limited time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people are tense when they have to write and it's still in English and we are not so in English. But I passed the examination. But before I got the answer, because I could have failed so I would have to repeat, is all-- the first group. There were two groups-- or the first, or the second, or both? But I passed. But before I got the answer, I didn't know what will be the answer. I decided that myself-- also, it wasn't required for me to go to a hospital, but I said, five years out of real medicine, not a medicine book and no real medicine, what I did was, you know, a nurse could do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could cure the-- the TB. And in Stockholm, I found out that it exists penicillin. We didn't know anything. So I went to a hospital to be an intern, which is a hard thing for this age. I was around-- I was 39 years old at that time. And usually, an intern is 25 and I wanted to represent Europe in the best way I could. So I was always working so hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which hospital was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Greenpoint. I went to a city hospital. I didn't want to go to a [INAUDIBLE] hospital because over there you have to fill out orders which another doctor give you, and in a city hospital you have more freedom of doing things. And I wanted to learn and I didn't go to make money or whatever. I got $50 a month for it. this wasn't important. It was important, but-- so I worked a year in Greenpoint. And when I finished, so started now what I do. First of all, I came home and with household-- I don't know how to cook and other things. So my husband said, if you don't know, you-- you buy a cookbook. I said, I don't understand the cookbook. He said, you will buy-- look in the Webster. And I said, you will wait for dinner. All right. So everything smoothened out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I write a very-- got-- I took a course or not, and I got a job, it is a part time job only, for the well-baby clinic. So it was from 9:00 to 11:30. And I opened my office, which, you know, gradual. I was a newcomer. And I moved here in-- to Washington Heights-- in 1951. And so I living here and-- And you had your private practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, here. And I worked-- worked in the well baby clinic for 30 years. I have an-- here a certificate. And to say that it was a time, but our marriage was very perfect not only because I came to America and I became the doctor. This was important, but more important was the marriage. He was a very nice person, a warm person for anybody--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and very supportive because he had a hard time with me to study one, to study the second later to the hospital. He made joke. He said, my wife is this and this and who envies me with the whole thing? But he was very supportive. He was an ardent Zionist and no one I have-- when he died, I had very many letters of condolence that he was a valuable asset for Jewish people, for Jewish culture, and Jewish tradition and Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1975, he died. He got a stroke in 74 which he recovered almost completely. And he bent to somebody's funeral, but he died in the street and didn't even make the funeral. I mentioned that I went to plenty of crises, whatever. But I survive. I am electrocuted, but not the legs. I had electric shocks, but I am not electrocuted somehow. So this, I think, is whatever you have to ask me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw before some papers. You donated a hospital together with late husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Israel because you see--

speaker: interviewer
text: --I don't know how to mention it. I never was eager for money and for material thing and I didn't have the time to be eager because I studied and later was-- studied again in Poland. And later came the war. And after the war is for sure this doesn't mean to me anything. I look up to other values. And as much as my husband was so for Israel, he came one day and said, what about if we give a clinic in Israel? This will be-- and he said to me-- this will be the matzevah for my children. So I said, by all mean. And we have a clinic and it is written, in memory of my children. And he considered this his matzevah. And I was proud of it. Now, some people said, you didn't object? I said, just the opposite. This was the asset of my whole marriage, that he was such a person, and I supported him completely. And what happened to your brother, Isaac?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother? I brought him over here to New York and he lived here and he died in 1991-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --here. Did he have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't marry. Also through all the things that he was running and coming and going. He was a Jew on-- on Aryan paper, he wasn't French, he was-- so he did never marry. And he died, and I special looked up, in '91, the 11th of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you told me you go to a Bible class--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I take the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --every once in a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: --adult education. So here I go-- I worked three years after my husband died, I still go there. Yeah. I gave up my office when he recovered from the stroke. He got the stroke in '74. And he had good care and he was active. He was going to all kind of meetings, but he says, you know, now an hour counts more than before a year, a month. So I understood the message that he wanted we should be more. So but the-- the clinic in the morning I didn't want to give up because I said, this is my backbone. This has to hold me. In the morning before he get up, there was help here so I am back. And so it was. So three years after-- when he died I still worked three years, but I had to give it up because it was mandatory because of the age. That time 70 years, you had to stop working. So I started looking what to do and I joined here is an open mind in the Reform Temple, someone introduced me. So there is for once a week, three hours. One is Jewish history, one hour. The other is a current event or music, and the third hour is the Bible, which I have no background in the Bible at all, no religious. That I'm not, but my parents [INAUDIBLE] but the girl didn't need to know. I don't know. So I learned-- it's hard for me to remember, but I value all these things and I-- and later I joined another class, which also there is retired professionals in the neighborhood. We meet once a week in Fridays and discuss different topics, nothing particular. And at the present time, I was asked before whether I think that we should talk about it, that the next generation should know what happened. So I just wanted to mention that we should absolutely talk about it not because there is nothing to brag what happened but first of all, we should believe was that that's it's true and this is the only way we can leave the message. And if-- if we-- and-- and everything what's happened is a lot of studies who is guilty, even in the ghetto and all the things which happen, because I know they are a discussion with our youth and elders, [INAUDIBLE] not. History will judge them. I am not the one who is going to judge. And now we've got concerns-- my personal life, you know, I lived long, I loved a lot of friends. Most of them are-- and I'm also easy to make friends, not just because a person talks is good enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's hard life, but-- and I was approached in the beginning before-- before-- for this different panels in the beginning after the war, but I wasn't ready emotionally. Physically, I am so more or less all right. But now I said, it's later than I think and if I don't do it now, so next year I might be not able or I might not be there. Well, thank you for giving me the honor to hear your story. Thank you very much. We'll look at some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to look at--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a translation of the original text of the diploma that I passed all the requirement necessary in our examination to receive the title of Doctor of Medicine in Paris. And this was in June, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a copy and translate-- a translation of the certificate I got from Stockholm, from the laboratory where I worked, signed by Professor Erik Wolff. I worked in the forensic medicine laboratory doing a special test.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture, Dr. Fox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I am-- this is my picture after the liberation when I was in Sweden in 1945-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --'46 because this is-- I recovered already a little from everything. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture with my husband when I came to America. It must have been-- I came to America in May '47, so a little bit later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yehuda Fox, the Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And none of his first family survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He-- his-- his family-- his wife and two children didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And none of your immediate family survived, either.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother from France, which he was here and you-- I mentioned that, yeah. I can go?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband and myself, we donated to a clinic in Israel, in Ben-Dor, near Haifa, and this was the invitation which was sent out to local people in Israel and friends to attend to the-- [INAUDIBLE], the opening. And this is the-- how it looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your husband call this clinic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we donated this clinic. My husband called it-- wanted that it should be the tombstone, the matzevah for his perished children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just describe what's in that picture, Dr. Fox? It's a plaque.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a plaque which is in the clinic on the wall saying that this is the-- the-- this clinic is in the memory of the children, Maria and Irene, of Julius Fox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were teenagers when they were murdered, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who were teenagers during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this diploma, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my diploma after I passed my State Board examination here in the New York, in America, in New York state.

speaker: interviewer
text: It licensed you to practice medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is the diploma which allows me to practice medicine. And you have one from Jewish-- from the hospital. This is a plaque which I received as recognition from-- there was a-- there is a medical alliance, Association of Jewish Physicians, from Poland, which at the present time we changed the name. It is called Janusz Korczak Medical Association. And I was-- I served as president for the year 1962 and 1963, which the term was two years. But later I was drafted for another year in 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my certificate that I served as an intern in a city hospital in New York, Greenpoint in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the certificate of service with the City of New York Department of Health. I-- this says that I worked efficiently, whatever, for 20 years. But I really worked for 30 years and I had to give it up. It was a mandatory resignation because of age.
